speaker: interviewer
text: --that before you left your home, when the Nazis came to Tuczyn--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that General Koch--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, General Koch was known as one of the biggest murderers in the world. He was killing Jewish people. And he was a terrible Nazi. And when they came to-- when they entered the city, they were looking for house to stay that had water. So they found our house and decided to stay in our house. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they pick your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was a beautiful house and had gardens and-- and fruit and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what else was beautiful about the house itself. Inside the house, what was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was many rooms. And the floor was sort of painted in red.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the walls? What was on the walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just usual nice walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Painted or wallpaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painted white.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there pictures, decoration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the pictures of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a piano?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a piano, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your piano like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a brand new piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: An upright? Or a-- a grand

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A normal sized piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else was in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usual furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your room, what did you have in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bed and a piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: The piano was in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, school books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many windows to the garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Many windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm in the winter in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: By making a fire with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a wood stove or a-- a fireplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fireplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there carpets on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about General Koch coming to take over your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the General of the Nazi army and General Koch. And then came a lot of leaders. Bormann came. All the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bormann came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bormann came. All those big shot Nazis came. And they said, we're going to kill all the Jewish people. And the generals-- the general of the Nazi army was sort of nice. He told them not to say that and all that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he, the general?

speaker: interviewee
text: General of the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a month, they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he--

speaker: interviewee
text: The front was going further.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living there along with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have meals together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to serve them. We used to make-- we made them pancakes. They liked very much pancakes the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to cook for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have to, but we made them pancakes. My mother made them pancakes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- [LAUGHS] --your job was what? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't do much, because I was a little girl only 10 year old. We were just talking to General Koch and all those Nazis. Why are they killing Jewish people and all that? And they-- they just said, that's the law and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They treat me nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could speak German with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring you any candies or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that nice, candy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But they weren't cruel to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In general, the Nazis didn't bother anybody until that time came on such and such date to kill. They didn't bother nobody. Even other Jewish people they didn't bother. They were nice to them. But then when the date came, they had orders to killed. they selected them and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they looked like in their uniforms? What did it look like to a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they all had their uniforms and their-- their crosses, their medals. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they were looking for partisans in the woods. They were sending out a lot of Nazis to look in the woods for partisans. They found some three soldiers and killed them. I don't know if they were Russian or Polish. They found three soldiers in the woods, and brought them to our house and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Killed them at your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near-- on the outside the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, they just give three shots and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were constantly-- they didn't find any more people in the woods except those three soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they killed those three, did they put the bodies--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --somewhere for people to see? You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever seen people shoot before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't see. Use-- be-- we weren't used to such cruelties.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very frightening for you as a little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Nazis who stayed in your home-- was there drinking at times?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had mostly their own food. The General usually stayed in the living room and draw-- and looked overs at the map, how was going to Russia. [LAUGHS] He was figuring maybe in one day, he'll take all Russia. That's what I imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sound pretty serious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The General always looked at the map, studying the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said they stayed about a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they moved further. Pardon me. Because the front was going further to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, you remained in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They remained until they didn't start to take the Jewish people to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you knew you were leaving your house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you take anything with you that he wanted to have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] [? We are-- ?] we could run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people took money or gold or valuables. Were you able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money wasn't-- money wasn't an issue at the time. Because there was no money. There was just German marks. And nobody wanted those German marks. You couldn't buy nothing with a German mark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Polish currency was out the fashion, out of value.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother take anything from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you left, was that house sealed? Were you able to go back to the house during the time you were in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were a long distance away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You witnessed Jews being taken before you left Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You wit-- you witnessed Jews being taken away before you left Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we left Tuczyn, they were saying they were taking the dentists and doctors to work. And they were selecting-- they were looking for dentists and doctors, Jewish doctors. They were saying they're taking them to work. But people were saying that they took them outside the city and shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them being taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They're taking all kinds of people to work, they were saying. I-- that-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you saw them being taken? What-- what did you actually see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually didn't see nothing. But I know that there were a lot of Jewish people were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they gave orders to select Jewish doctors, Jewish dentists, to work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone told you about that? Or, how did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish committee. And the Jewish committee had to give people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat, yeah. Right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any members of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: My girlfriend's father was the President of the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwartzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that mean that you knew more about what was happening through her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody knew. It was a small city. And everybody knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews do you think there were in Tucyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about 5,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Mr Schwartzman and--

speaker: interviewee
text: He got killed. His two daughters survived. Their down in Israel. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend was which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: One girl was with me in the same grade in school. And the sister was older, two years. They're now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwartzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbara and Mani Schwartzman, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're still friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I visit them in Israel. They survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was their father killed, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to the ghetto. But they made a uprising in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her Father gave a lot of money to buy ammunition and all kinds of stuff to fight against the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the Tuczyn ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you know about what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I wasn't in the ghetto. But I know that they were fighting back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the girls manage to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the papers done [? there ?] to Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had false papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family ever considered getting false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you know about the Judenrat in Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they worked for the Nazis. And the Nazis always ordered, they wanted money. Sometimes they wanted valuables. Sometimes they wanted people. to say they're taking them to work. Of course, they were killing them. And that's what all what the Judenrat did, collecting valuables from Jewish people, giving it to the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they collect from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They collect from everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having to give up valuables of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know about that. My mother was dealing with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to being in the woods, the people that you were with in the woods all survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't survive. A day before the liberation some-- some-- some soldiers were killing the Jewish people. It wasn't the Nazis, it was other soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of-- a lot of the Bandera group, those were fighting for independence Ukraine. Some of those Bandera group were killing Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why would they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That's a good question. It's-- they were doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was killed in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some young people, some older people. That was a day before the liberation. A lot of Jewish people got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what day would that have been?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw people killed by the Bandera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, we-- we knew that they were killed, so we imagine it was the Bandera group, because they were killing Jewish people. But we actually don't know which partisans killed them. Some partisans killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were found killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to [INAUDIBLE], and then we met-- were met by the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. It's a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. And tell me about seeing the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the leader of the Russian army was a Jewish general. He was half Jewish, half Russian, Boris Lebdavidov. And he-- he liberated us. I was talking to him and everything. And then when I was in Germany after the war, I read the paper that Boris Lebdavidov got kill, in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He liberated Berlin. And he got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you spoke with him, was it in Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew Russian as well as Polish and German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because in 1939, the Russians took over the part of Poland that I was living in. I was going to a Russian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: We haven't talked-- we talked a little bit about school-- but about the higher grades. How far did you go in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to grade seven. And then I was a year in gymnasium. And then I was-- then I made exams for grade 9 and 10, and then I had one year university.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study in university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, if there had not been a war, what would your occupation have been do you think? What would you have done?

speaker: interviewee
text: A teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you have liked to teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to study again after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I wasn't studying any more, just in Russia this-- this [INAUDIBLE] that I was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians liberated you and your aunt, your cousin, your mother, anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my uncle. My mother had two sisters. They both survived the war. And my uncle survived the war. He was in the woods. He was in a hide out at the Polish farmer's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he in a different place?

speaker: interviewee
text: You just preferred not to be in the woods. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the woods was a very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you that were in the woods ever think of giving up, because it was so hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had no choice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there was a war between the Ukrainian and Polish people. So the Polish people left for Poland. The Ukrainian people stayed right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: While the war was going on. Ukrainian and Polish were fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had nothing to do with us. The Polish people wanted us to come with them and to cross the river and go over to-- on the Russian side. But we were afraid to go with them. Maybe some of the Polish people will give us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't trust them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were all kinds of Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were liberated, you were then how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, around the 11, 12-- 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was living in Tuczyn. I was going to university, and then I came back to Tuzcyn. And then we went to Poland. And from Poland, we went to Germany. And from Germany, we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your mother returned to the home that you had lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we didn't live there. We moved to the city where-- where the government has their offices.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody that survived lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to live in the country? Was there a reason not to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a reason not to live in the suburb where you had lived before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not particularly. Because the other survivors stayed in the city, too. So we stayed in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did go back and see your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it had been emptied out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that, about visiting, seeing it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] it was just too bad nothing was left. They took everything out of the house. I was think that was there was food-- was all kinds of food and all kinds of things. They took it all. They emptied it all out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Nazi-- the Nazi police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not local people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in the city of Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house. There were so many empty houses, [LAUGHS] you could move into any house. There was houses and no people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews came back to Tuczyn do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, quite a few, some young girls, some-- the Mani Schwartzman came back and Barbara Schwartzman with the-- the [? praises ?] of the Judenrat children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have mostly Jewish friends at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there German young people again? Or, they had all gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: All gone to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Polish young people? Did you have friends among them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Polish people weren't there, too. It was just Ukrainian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- were your friends some of them Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have from that time when you were a student after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were going to school. And there were a lot of Ukrainian students, some were Russian girls. Some girls came from Russia to study, Moscow from Leningrad to study at this university.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time in your life, were you thinking still of going to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do anything to make that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we decided to go when-- it came a lot that all the Polish citizens before the war are afraid to go to Poland. So we went to Poland. From Poland, we went to Germany. From Germany, we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think to stay in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because mostly Jewish people were emigrating to-- to Canada, the states or Israel. So, I mean, everybody is doing what everybody else is doing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think to go to Israel after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We wanted to go to Canada, because my mother had a brother here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he able to sponsor you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still single at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came by yourself to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came with my mother and stepfather and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flin Flon, Manitoba.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like arriving in Flin Flon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very nice. We worked at the uncle's store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your impressions were of Canadians?

speaker: interviewee
text: A swell, friendly people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to learn English when you came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew a little English from the gymnasium. I studied after when I went to-- in Europe. And I studied privately English in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any French?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
